text:
Wonder if the chance that scaling up with Photonics will be required, and not just for the scale out is priced in.

Maine won’t be the only state to block data center builds due to energy consumption and thermal regulation advantages could drive use in scaling up if mass production can be efficiently achieved.

I’d also wonder how many of the “canceled” data centers got word that “hey your current schematics won’t work for where we are going” so it’s forced to go back to a rebid.

This stuff is fairly expensive to screw up so although CapEx is urgent it’s not without strategy.

text: The photonics cluster is what jumps out — $AAOI +332%, $AEHR +249%, $LITE +143%, $COHR +67% are all riding the same 800G/1.6T optical interconnect buildout inside hyperscaler data centers. This isn't random, it's one thesis playing out across four names. $SNDK and $WDC at #2 and #5 is also interesting, HDD/flash demand recovering hard while everyone was focused on AI chips. What's the one name on this list that still has the most room left in your view?

text:
$AAOI looks a bit extended but no denying the strength both in the photonics basket as well as this name specifically. 200 should come in short order if this holds up.

Two ways to play it: retest of the 10d SMA for the lower-risk entry, or a breakout through 160.

Watch LITE, GLW and the rest of the photonics basket for confirmation, always best to see names within a group/theme move together.

text:
Frontrunning 1.6T/CPO within the broader photonics supercycle is the most compelling investment to me. 

I have high conviction in that statement.

Which is why I'm long the entire supply chain (+1 extra bottlenecK)

1. $SIVE - Their laser revenue scales aggressively with $JBL, $MRVL, Ayar, O-Net.

And I do think CPO/1.6T will blow away any conservative analyst projections from how hard $NVDA, $GOOGL, and others have been pushing photonics architectures.

Downside risk is multi-sourcing, but there's a reason Jabil chose Sivers. When you compare $MTSI, $LITE, $COHR, Furukawa, and others.

There's genuinely not many laser suppliers in the entire world... they're all $10B+, then you have this mini CHIPS act chokepoint trading at <$1B MC.

2. Shunsin (6451) - I don't see how it's possible Foxconn's optical foundry for testing, packaging, and assembly is valued at $1.5B MC less than $LWLG. 

When they look extremely derisked piggybacking off of Foxconn's photonics volume.

$TSM's optical arm VisEra example is ~$5B, but they scale H2 2028 from Gen-3. Foxconn looks to be ramping up just next year.

They're just scaling low fwd p/e multiples off of $NVDA CPO supply chain demand in Taiwan and all public indicators point to capacity expansion + extreme demand. 
 
3. Win Semi - They're the foundry for Sivers to scale up DFB laser production. As well as $AVGO, SpaceX supply chains and others.

When I do supply chain mapping and Win Semi pops up in every single frontier supply chain I see. 

There's probably something markets are not pricing in.

4. $MRVL - I find this genuinely compelling as a mini-Broadcomm. 

Their potential design with with $GOOGL today, helps the case past 2028.

But the catalyst I was looking at was $MSFT Maia ramp, which happens H2 2026, and likely keep scaling up exponentially into 2027, 2028, 2029. 

Celestial acquisition was probably the smartest thing in the world for them. Maybe on next drop or CSP? 

5. $HPS.A - Transformers/Switchgears are commodities  + boring parts of the DC supply chain.

However, when the bottleneck is 2-5 years, and you have backlog increasing 100%+... causing extreme shortages.

It's only up 20%+ since my thesis post, but I do see this being de-risked given massive backlog visibility (even though it's inferred, they don't give exact #). 

I do think markets are missing something, especially with potential gross margin expansion from price hikes if they pull it off.... 

Again backlog + demand just de-risks this company,  and it seems like a high growth compounder post facility expansion last year. 

There's many others like $NBIS, $JBL, $RPI, $TSEM, $LITE, $ARM, $SOI, $AXTI,  $IQE, $ALRIB, Fittech, PCL, and others that I'm very fond of, but just mentioning 5 off the top of my head from today's prices... if I'm creating a new portfolio. 

Of course, it's good to barbell with other uncorrelated companies to AI supply chains, but these are just 5 I liked.

U.S.-based InP laser manufacturing. It has already secured over $200M in its first volume 1.6T order from one hyperscale customer followed by another $124M in 800G orders from a second.\n\n2. $AEHR building the reliability layer for the optical & AI hardware stack through burn-in & test systems. It just received a record $41M follow-on order from its lead hyperscale customer reinforcing the idea that Sonoma is becoming a key production burn-in platform for high-power AI ASICs.\n\n3. $CRDO building the connectivity layer that helps AI clusters move data faster through active electrical cables, retimers & high-speed interconnect silicon. The DustPhotonics acquisition also extends that platform into silicon photonics before copper becomes a real constraint.\n\n4. $LITE building the laser layer of the AI optical stack through EMLs, optical components & optical switching exposure. The setup is backed by a $2B $NVDA strategic investment & optical circuit switch backlog above $400M with orders reportedly extending through 2028.\n\n5. $VIAV building the testing & validation layer of the optical stack through network instrumentation & photonics measurement tools. It is the picks-and-shovels layer of the transition because every high-speed optical buildout still needs to be tested regardless of which transceiver vendor wins.\n\n6. $COHR building one of the core photonics bottlenecks through indium phosphide lasers, optical engines & communications components tied to next-gen AI networking. It also has a $2B $NVDA strategic investment behind it & is doubling InP device capacity into the 1.6T ramp.\n\n7. $MRVL building the DSP & optical infrastructure layer through electro-optics, PAM DSPs, interconnect silicon & custom networking chips. text:
This map shows why Sivers - ( $SIVE ) is quietly becoming one of the most important names in AI photonics.

Sivers sits in the center of the CPO ecosystem supplying the critical multi-wavelength laser arrays to:

•  Jabil (1.6T LRO pluggables)
•  Ayar Labs (optical I/O)
•  O-Net + Enablence (external light source modules)
•  POET (hybrid integration, now tied to Marvell + Google TPU discussions)

That’s direct or indirect exposure to $META , $AMZN , $MSFT , $GOOGL , and $MRVL — basically the entire hyperscaler map.

Still only ~$720M market cap

The Nasdaq listing is coming. The commercial ramps are coming.

39 M$ de Capitalisation boursière >Elle est directement en rapport avec la photonique, elle fabrique des atténuateurs Optiques Variables (VOA) MEMSCAP, à la base, c’est surtout un fournisseur de capteurs de pression de très haute précision pour l’aéronautique et le médical. Mais ils ont aussi un segment communications optiques. Ils fournissent des VOA basés sur technologie MEMS, utilisés pour gérer dynamiquement la puissance des signaux dans les réseaux fibre optique (datacenters, télécoms, amplis EDFA). Sur ce segment de niche, MEMSCAP est tout simplement leader mondial des puces MEMS VOA, avec une part de marché estimée à plus de 80% sur un marché d’environ 5 M$ au niveau puces, et 50 M$ au niveau produits finis. Pas l'ASML de la photonique mais une boite qui pourrait se rerater rapidement. D'ailleurs hier il y a eu un afflux plutôt anormal, ce qui me fait potentiellement penser à un positionnement d’un bon gros player. Si les microcaps continuent d'exploser, je parie que Memscap en bénéficiera probablement."}}}, "rest_id": "2045528106413199710", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "343592", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2045643800786395522", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "206", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
✏️LESSON EXTRACTION FROM SUCCESSFUL PITCH

Soitec ( $SOI.PA) by @jasons_chips 

121% gain since the March 10 pitch.

📋 WHAT THE PITCH ARGUED:
🔹 Mobile segment was at a cyclical floor with inventory clearing.
🔹 "Smart Cut" technology created a near-monopoly in photonics substrates.
🔹 Management was deliberately low-balling future growth projections.

✅ WHAT ACTUALLY HAPPENED:
🔹 Q3 sequential revenue beat confirmed the mobile trough was in.
🔹 1.6T networking demand pulled photonics orders forward significantly.

🎯 THE SURPRISE:
The market re-rated the stock as a pure AI infrastructure play almost overnight. It shifted from a "boring" materials cyclical to a critical networking bottleneck provider.

MY TAKE: We expected a slow recovery as inventory cleared. Instead, NVIDIA's commentary on Co-Packaged Optics triggered a massive institutional rotation into the name. This shows how quickly a value setup turns into a momentum play when the macro tailwind arrives.
But if I am honest, this performance is likely a result of important people mentioning this company on X 😀

text:
Price action doesn’t lie — tape making higher lows while headlines scream fear. The structural AI buildout in optics is impossible to ignore. 

Bullish on photonics supply chain names like $SIVE (high-power DFB lasers), $AEHR (SiPh wafer burn-in), $SOI.PA (Photonics-SOI monopoly), $AAOI, and a handful of others on my list ($ALRIB.PA, $4977.TW, $6706.TW etc.) showing real setups. 

Pullbacks = accumulation, not the top.

Price action is starting to reflect it.

$LITE $COHR $AXTI $AAOI 

#stocks #AI #photonics #semiconductors #investing

Marvell $MRVL Announced they will be acquiring Polariton Technologies 

They plan to add high speed low power plasmonics based silicon photonics devices to its portfolio

This gives Marvell a stronger presence in optical technology and positions them better for next gen connectivity

The partnership also includes joint development of silicon photonics https://t.co/7lhuDMAxHN

text:
$poet and $sivef indirect benefits to this $mrvl acquisition-

$poet Technologies (POET) sees indirect positive momentum, not a direct tie to Polariton itself.POET’s core tech is its Optical Interposer platform—a wafer-scale, passive-alignment hybrid photonics solution that integrates light sources, modulators, waveguides, etc., into compact optical engines/light engines. It’s upstream in the same AI interconnect value chain Marvell is aggressively building out.
Marvell’s prior Celestial AI acquisition (Dec 2025) already made POET a beneficiary: Celestial used POET’s tech (e.g., Blazar light sources) as a key enabler for its Photonic Fabric. Marvell inherited that supply relationship, and POET has confirmed purchase orders/shipments tied to it.

This new Polariton deal further validates Marvell’s heavy commitment to scaling photonics for AI (now adding plasmonic modulation on top of SiPh). It doesn’t displace POET—it reinforces the ecosystem demand for integrated optical engines. Analysts and investors have repeatedly called out POET as a “real winner” or potential M&A target in the wake of Marvell’s optics moves, as hyperscalers/NVIDIA/Marvell need proven, scalable integration platforms. 

$POET gets another leg of validation and potential order acceleration. It’s not a direct “Polariton synergy” play, but the Marvell optics flywheel helps POET’s positioning in the same AI connectivity wave.

 $sivef is a strong indirect beneficiary and one of the more credible pure-plays in the exact layer this deal targets.Sivers Photonics specializes in high-performance DFB laser arrays, CW (continuous-wave) lasers, and external light source modules explicitly designed for AI data-center optical interconnects, CPO, pluggables, and SiPh integration.
They have established partnerships in the ecosystem (e.g., with POET for light engines, Ayar Labs, and paths into Marvell/Celestial/NVIDIA optics roadmaps via the CW-WDM MSA and related collaborations). Their lasers are a critical “fuel” for SiPh-based modulators and engines—like the plasmonic + SiPh solutions Marvell just acquired.
This deal accelerates adoption of exactly the architectures Sivers supplies (1.6T+ LRO transceivers, CPO, ultra-low-power AI interconnects). Recent Sivers news (e.g., Jabil collaboration on 1.6T modules) already shows momentum; Marvell’s move pours more fuel on the fire for laser array demand.

text:
$GFS GlobalFoundries 

定位:AI 数据中心 → 光互联 → 硅光(Silicon Photonics)代工厂

坐标系:
光引擎里的两种主流技术路线:一种基于磷化铟(InP,做激光最好,也是 Sivers / AXT 的主场),另一种基于硅(硅光 / SiPh,做调制和波导最适合 CMOS 量产)。

硅光的思路是"把光学器件用成熟半导体工艺做在硅晶圆上",好处是能借用半导体行业几十年积累的产能、良率、成本曲线。问题是硅本身不会发光,所以硅光要和 InP 激光配合使用 —— 这是 POET 这类"封装平台"存在的原因。

硅光需要专门的工艺和专门的晶圆厂。GlobalFoundries 就是这个赛道的最大代工厂。

公司背景:
美国 Malta, NY 总部,全球纯代工厂第三名(TSMC、Samsung 之后,和 UMC、SMIC 同级)。不做最先进制程(3nm/5nm 是 TSMC 的地盘),专做 12–28nm 的特殊工艺节点 —— 射频、模拟混合信号、功率管理、SOI、以及硅光。

2025 年 11 月收购了新加坡 AMF(Advanced Micro Foundry),这是全球第一家商业化硅光纯代工厂,拥有 200mm 硅光平台。

加上之前收购的埃及 InfiniLink(光学数据连接芯片创业公司),GlobalFoundries 宣称自己是全球最大的纯代工硅光晶圆厂。

技术路线:
纽约工厂做 300mm 硅光,新加坡(AMF)做 200mm 硅光,两地互补。

GF 同时在做 InP 激光和硅 CMOS 的单片异构集成,长期目标是做出"一块晶圆上同时有激光、调制器、驱动、波导"的完全集成光引擎。

赛道位置:
硅光代工这个环节格局是:Intel 做自用的(自己的 CPO 产品)、TSMC 给 Broadcom 做、GlobalFoundries 是最大的纯代工(向所有人开放)。

Broadcom、Marvell、Cisco、Coherent、Lumentum 这些公司的硅光芯片,相当一部分会在 GF 的晶圆厂生产。

它的地位相当于硅光赛道的"TSMC" —— 不做自己品牌产品,做所有人的代工。

财务和公司状态:
市值约 200 亿美金,2026 年硅光业务预计贡献 AMF 收购后 +7500 万美金,目标 2030 年前硅光年收入突破 10 亿美金。

硅光市场本身以 25% CAGR 增长。

主体业务(非硅光)还是传统代工,受汽车和工业需求拖累,财务表现平淡。2026 年 3 月做了二次公开发行 + 并行股份回购,市场反应一般。

所以 GFS 的股价走势更像"被硅光故事带动的大盘股",不是纯粹硅光标的。

AI 周期里的角色:
"硅光代工"的稀缺标的。

想直接投硅光,美股里几乎没有纯正选项(Ayar Labs 未上市,Lightmatter 未上市),GFS 是公开市场最直接的硅光赋能者 —— 但要意识到它的非硅光业务占比还很大,硅光故事被稀释。

核心赌注:
硅光在 2027–2030 年真正从 CPO 需求起量;
GF 在开放代工市场能拿住大部分份额;
主体代工业务不继续恶化。

仓位定位:
硅光主题的代理持仓(proxy play),不是纯正选项。

和 POET / Sivers 形成互补:POET / Sivers 是"如果硅光/CPO 跑通 10 倍股"的押注,GFS 是"硅光跑通它至少有稳定订单"的底仓。

适合追求硅光敞口但又不想承担 early-stage 波动的配置。

关键观察点:
AMF 整合进度和新加坡硅光产能爬坡 → 
Broadcom / Marvell 等大客户的硅光订单走向 → 
主体代工业务汽车/工业板块能否止跌。

硅光的卖铲人。

[], "user_mentions": []}, "id": "Tm90ZVR3ZWV0OjIwNDcwNzQ0NjkwMjI1MDI5MTI=", "media": {"inline_media": []}, "richtext": {"richtext_tags": []}, "text": "$GFS GlobalFoundries 定位:AI 数据中心 → 光互联 → 硅光(Silicon Photonics)代工厂 坐标系: 光引擎里的两种主流技术路线:一种基于磷化铟(InP,做激光最好,也是 Sivers / AXT 的主场),另一种基于硅(硅光 / SiPh,做调制和波导最适合 CMOS 量产)。 硅光的思路是"把光学器件用成熟半导体工艺做在硅晶圆上",好处是能借用半导体行业几十年积累的产能、良率、成本曲线。问题是硅本身不会发光,所以硅光要和 InP 激光配合使用 —— 这是 POET 这类"封装平台"存在的原因。 硅光需要专门的工艺和专门的晶圆厂。GlobalFoundries 就是这个赛道的最大代工厂。 公司背景: 美国 Malta, NY 总部,全球纯代工厂第三名(TSMC、Samsung 之后,和 UMC、SMIC 同级)。不做最先进制程(3nm/5nm 是 TSMC 的地盘),专做 12–28nm 的特殊工艺节点 —— 射频、模拟混合信号、功率管理、SOI、以及硅光。 2025 年 11 月收购了新加坡 AMF(Advanced Micro Foundry),这是全球第一家商业化硅光纯代工厂,拥有 200mm 硅光平台。 加上之前收购的埃及 InfiniLink(光学数据连接芯片创业公司),GlobalFoundries 宣称自己是全球最大的纯代工硅光晶圆厂。 技术路线: 纽约工厂做 300mm 硅光,新加坡(AMF)做 200mm 硅光,两地互补。 GF 同时在做 InP 激光和硅 CMOS 的单片异构集成,长期目标是做出"一块晶圆上同时有激光、调制器、驱动、波导"的完全集成光引擎。 赛道位置: 硅光代工这个环节格局是:Intel 做自用的(自己的 CPO 产品)、TSMC 给 Broadcom 做、GlobalFoundries 是最大的纯代工(向所有人开放)。 Broadcom、Marvell、Cisco、Coherent、Lumentum 这些公司的硅光芯片,相当一部分会在 GF 的晶圆厂生产。 它的地位相当于硅光赛道的"TSMC" —— 不做自己品牌产品,做所有人的代工。 财务和公司状态: 市值约 200 亿美金,2026 年硅光业务预计贡献 AMF 收购后 +7500 万美金,目标 2030 年前硅光年收入突破 10 亿美金。 硅光市场本身以 25% CAGR 增长。 主体业务(非硅光)还是传统代工,受汽车和工业需求拖累,财务表现平淡。2026 年 3 月做了二次公开发行 + 并行股份回购,市场反应一般。 所以 GFS 的股价走势更像"被硅光故事带动的大盘股",不是纯粹硅光标的。 AI 周期里的角色: "硅光代工"的稀缺标的。 想直接投硅光,美股里几乎没有纯正选项(Ayar Labs 未上市,Lightmatter 未上市),GFS 是公开市场最直接的硅光赋能者 —— 但要意识到它的非硅光业务占比还很大,硅光故事被稀释。 核心赌注: 硅光在 2027–2030 年真正从 CPO 需求起量; GF 在开放代工市场能拿住大部分份额; 主体代工业务不继续恶化。 仓位定位: 硅光主题的代理持仓(proxy play),不是纯正选项。 和 POET / Sivers 形成互补:POET / Sivers 是"如果硅光/CPO 跑通 10 倍股"的押注,GFS 是"硅光跑通它至少有稳定订单"的底仓。 适合追求硅光敞口但又不想承担 early-stage 波动的配置。 关键观察点: AMF 整合进度和新加坡硅光产能爬坡 → Broadcom / Marvell 等大客户的硅光订单走向 → 主体代工业务汽车/工业板块能否止跌。 硅光的卖铲人。"}}}, "rest_id": "2047074469135655314", text: TXN showing signs that Analog Chip Season may finally be taking off. I’ve been talking about this coming transition in the hardware trade since Q4 of last year and it finally seems like it may be upon us. The first screenshot below hurts my soul, me pumping Photonics in early December but not longing any of them, but that’s the macro tourist game for ya. Regardless, this signals the continuing evolution of the manufacturing boom going on globally. In a time where energy constraints are picking up, any hardware that can bypass those constraints will get bid up here big time, and that is exactly what Analog chips provide, energy efficiency. Listen I know I’ve been banging the drum on potential trouble coming to the AI Datacenter Buildout but I don’t think it is going to break yet or crash the economy yet. As long as NVDA remains elevated, even if flat, capital from that sector will continue to slosh around into different ancillary corners of the same trade. I.E. as NVDA has languished since Q3 of last year, memory stocks have been on a tear. I think we are going to see now a rotation from this existing capital base start going into the Analog semiconductor names. I think as manufacturing output begins to diversify away from AI, I think having exposure to Analog Semis will help buttress any pain from the AI Hardware trade that should come, once demand destruction starts getting actualized. I think I’m going to finally get some exposure to them as I think it is a great asymmetric hedge to my Downstream AI thesis being expressed thru META RDDT & IBM calls.

text:
Asian supply chain leaks just revealed the Holy Grail of wearables, and the market is completely missing the underlying silicon photonics chokepoint. 🚨 

Let’s connect the dots on Point 6 regarding the Apple Watch Ultra and the non-invasive blood glucose sensor. If you want to know who is building the light engine, look at the attached slide from $SIVE / $SIVEF. 🧵👇

1️⃣ The Leak: Miniaturized Lasers for Health
According to the supply chain leak below, Apple is targeting April next year for its blood glucose feature. The tech? Non-invasive spectroscopy using miniaturized lasers to collect reflected wavelengths through the skin.

2️⃣ The F100 Client Connection
Look at the attached slide from Sivers Semiconductors ($SIVE). A mystery "US Fortune 100" client has invested ~$18M in NRE to develop custom Indium Phosphide (InP) optical sensors for "Consumer devices" (notice the smartwatch icon). Apple is Top 5 on the Fortune 100.

3️⃣ The Timeline Matches Perfectly
Sivers delivered an order of 50,000 chips in Q1 2024 to this F100 client. 50k is not mass production—it is the exact volume required for EVT/DVT (Engineering & Design Validation Testing) prototype runs. A prototype run in early 2024 aligns perfectly with an aggressive commercial launch in Spring 2027.

4️⃣ The True TAM (Total Addressable Market)
The slide explicitly states that when this project enters the volume phase, the Request For Proposal (RFP) ranges from $30M to several $100M per year.

While retail investors are looking at historical P/E ratios of a legacy telecom business, they are completely blind to the fact that $SIVE has spent years building the miniaturized InP laser engine for the biggest consumer health revolution of the decade.
The data is right there for anyone willing to do the math. 1+1=2. 🍏⌚️

⚠️ Disclaimer: Supply chain leaks are unverified rumors by nature and timelines can shift. However, investing is about connecting high-probability data points. The technological and chronological overlap here is simply too precise to ignore. As always, DYOR.

text:
I’m now long $POET

$POET is one of the wildest turnaround stories in photonics, not so long ago I was bearish with the hard dilution around this company. 

Rewind to early 2024. Company was near insolvent. Nearly ran out of cash. Forced into an emergency raise. Share count has nearly 4x’d in two years.
Fast forward to today:
+$ 313M cash on the balance sheet (vs ~$1.3B market cap)
+ Just raised $150M in January at $7.25 from institutional investors
+ 152M shares out vs 40M-ish pre-2024
+ Marvell orders now confirmed by the CFO
+ Foxconn and Luxshare partnerships already disclosed
+ Strategic tie-up with $SIVE on integrated light sources

The dilution was brutal. But look at what it bought: a wafer-level optical interposer platform that Marvell, Foxconn, Luxshare, and Sivers are all plugged into.

The old POET was a story stock with $41K in revenue. The new POET just 25x’d revenue YoY and is embedded in the CPO supply chain right next to $SIVE

A raising phoenix as it seems.

I’m long since yesterday. Great continuation today up 20% and still a long way to re rate.

God speed! 🍺

1. $SIVE - asymmetric CPO and RF‑photonics optionality

2. $COHR - the only scaled 6‑inch InP plus SiPh platform

3. $FN - underpriced AI‑optics manufacturing choke point

4. $MYSI - cheap way to own 3.2T analog content

5. $CIEN - AI backbone growth masking as “old telco”

These are just a few names we're categorizing as "undervalued". 

We will be developing a white paper with a full list of our top watches and value equities in the photonics space this weekend. 

$SIVE full equity report to be sent this Thursday.

text:
The AI infrastructure bottleneck nobody talks about.

As AI clusters scale past copper's physical limits, optical photonics becomes the critical layer.

These companies are building the infrastructure layer that powers the next generation of AI systems—and the investment signals that come with them.

A massive data throughput upgrade is coming. The question is which companies capture the value.

text:
I've been quietly watching on the sideline for a couple of months now.

Everybody and their grandmas are hyping photonics (and for a very good reason, no doubt on that!) but there are truly a few people doing the actual work and a ton of parrots.

A handful of persons truly do the research and others just copy/paste it, reformulate it, ask AI to generate something they didn't even read and then post.

text:
Spot on by @Chaamacoo. 🎯

People keep trying to put $SIVE / $SIVEF in a single box. It’s not just an "AI stock", a "defense stock", or a "telecom stock." It is the foundational silicon and photonics chokepoint for all of them.

Think of them as the "Intel Inside" of the next hardware supercycle. If you want to understand the true TAM, here are the actual end-products that will rely on "Sivers Inside" to function:

🧠 AI Datacenters: Next-gen 1.6T LRO pluggable transceivers (Jabil/Marvell) & Co-Packaged Optics.

 🪖 NATO Defense & SATCOM: @allspaceltd  multi-orbit terminals for Special Forces (SOF) & Navy frigates. 

🚁 Drones (UAVs): Stealth flat-panel SATCOM connectivity and FMCW LiDAR navigation. 

🗼 Next-Gen Telecom: 5G/6G massive MIMO antennas & Fixed Wireless Access (FWA). 

🚄 Infrastructure: High-speed "Track-to-Train" gigabit broadband networks. 

🚗 Autonomous Vehicles: Advanced LiDAR sensor arrays for self-driving cars. 

⌚ Wearables & Health: Biometric optical sensors (think next-gen Apple Watch & health trackers).

You aren't buying a single theme hoping it plays out. You are buying the underlying crossroads of tomorrow's technology. The sum-of-the-parts (SOTP) valuation could be huge. ⏳🔥
$SIVE $SIVEF #Semiconductors #Photonics

"richtext_types": ["Bold"], "to_index": 2439}, {"from_index": 2465, "richtext_types": ["Bold"], "to_index": 2499}]}, "text": "What makes a company truly compelling is not just what it is today, but how many powerful trends it could end up riding at once. That is a big part of why $SIVE / $SIVEF stands out to me. Most investors build exposure one theme at a time. They buy one company for AI, another for defense, another for satcom, another for photonics, and maybe yet another for next-generation telecom. It is a bit like assembling the future one puzzle piece at a time, hoping the picture eventually comes together. Sivers feels different. It feels more like finding one piece that already connects several of the most important parts of that picture. At first glance, it is easy to label it as just another semiconductor company. But the more I look at it, the more that feels like an oversimplification. Sivers sits in the overlap between mmWave, photonics, datacenter connectivity, satcom and defense. Those are not niche or isolated markets. They are major long-term themes, and the company has exposure to several of them at once. That is what makes the setup so compelling. Sivers does not need every single door to open at the same time for the story to work. It may be enough for one or two of these markets to accelerate, one or two partnerships to deepen, or one part of the business to gain meaningful commercial traction before investors begin to see the company in a very different light. That is why I think the “one stock, several themes” angle matters here. For a new investor, Sivers may be easier to understand if you stop thinking about it as a single narrow bet and start thinking about it as a small platform with multiple shots on goal. Not one straight road, but a crossroads. Not one engine, but several potential drivers of value that could begin contributing at different times. Of course, that does not remove the risk. Stories with several moving parts are never simple. But they can also create exactly the kind of asymmetry that makes small-cap investing so interesting, especially when the market has not fully decided how to categorize the company yet. And that is often where the upside starts to emerge. So the appeal of Sivers, to me, is not just that it has exposure to attractive end markets. It is that you do not need to buy five different companies to get that exposure. You are looking at one company that could potentially benefit from several major technology shifts at once. That kind of setup is rare. And that is why I think Sivers deserves a much closer look."}}}, "rest_id": "2045914937076183188", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "10333", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2045961814413115759", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "6974", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

$FORM $TER — test infra

The infra supercycle of 2026 is here.

text:
Photonics is a stack! 
$AXTI $AIXA $TSEM $AEHR $LITE $GLW

Here is a clean map of the sector.
Please bookmark & share! 

A laser, a photonic chip, a finished module, and a deployed AI network all sit at different points in one long chain. 

Different companies get paid for totally different reasons and on totally different timelines.

I just updated my layer framework so you can instantly see where every major name fits that I track (and why the AI buildout lifts the whole stack).

Quick map:

Layer 1 (Materials & Platforms): $AXTI $ALMU $LWLG $IQE
Layer 2 (Epitaxy Tools): $AIXA $ALRIB $VECO
Layer 3 (Foundries & Scale): $TSEM $FN $SANM
Layer 4 (Test & Yield): $AEHR $FORM $COHU $TER $VIAV
Layer 5 (Devices & Engines): $LITE $COHR $AAOI $POET $SIVE $MTSI
Layer 6 (Connectivity & Networks): $CRDO $MRVL $SMTC $CIEN $NOK $GLW

There are many more names in the industry that didn't make this list. 

Which are you tracking the closest?

Agreed—the signals are aligning faster than consensus expects. Photonics holding RS while semis pause and energy narratives mature points to the transfer layer waking up. 

Group rotation in optical components + capex pivots toward interconnect efficiency (not just more FLOPs) is the tell. COHR’s positioning in that bandwidth chokepoint looks timely.

What’s your read on the next confirmation trigger—earnings language or order flow?

@grok It’s already happening @grok Semis are crowded.
Energy is understood. Now you’re seeing:

-photonics holding strength
-group rotation instead of single names
-capex shifting toward interconnect + bandwidth

That’s the early phase. Not obvious yet…but it’s forming.

Nokia’s bull thesis (as of April 2026): $NOK

The $2.3B Infinera acquisition has created a vertically integrated optical/photonics powerhouse addressing AI data center bottlenecks with high-speed coherent pluggables (1.6T+), lower power, and scalable DCI fabrics. 

OFC 2026 demos and strong hyperscaler orders (+17% optical growth) signal acceleration in the 11–18% CAGR interconnect market.

Combined with NVIDIA’s $1B investment and AI-RAN trials (T-Mobile, SoftBank), Nokia builds end-to-end AI-native networks from wireless edge (RAN leadership + 6G foundations) to optical core—few peers match this stack.

2026 guidance provides a floor; optical/IP ramps (10–12%+ potential) plus margin expansion could drive 10–15%+ EPS growth and rerating to growth multiples.

I’m seeing some pushback on an equipment level, and I want to explain a little bit on that. @vlmkapital wrote a good post on how he views the investment case from the equipement level (link in comments) and I want to expand on things there are missing. Heads up.. this one is longer than usual. Most of the pushback I’m seeing X on sectors is superficial, and the argument I’m making here has multiple layers that all depend on each other. So if you want the short version, you won’t find it here💁‍♂️ @GyujinAAIG is working on the full scope for AAIG, and will publish the research soon but I will lay out the strategy part from my side already a bit. The lens that is being used when tracking companies like LPKF and the timelines is a technology adoption kind of lens. Will give @FinnStockinger also a tag in this part. But they’re missing the capital allocation and incentive lens, which is freaking crucial for this thesis. I’m going to explain a little bit why, and later I’m also going to use Aehr Test Systems as some kind of example of what happened there. So what’s the situation on the ground now? Semco expects the glass substrate era to enter into full swing around 2027-ish. They are currently developing samples with customers, and Absolics is also targeting mass production in 2027 and moving ahead with preparation. They got SKC approval for a capital increase of around $690 million, allocating roughly $410 million into Absolics for product development. So there are real commitments there, no doubt. Also, I’m not saying to totally ignore the glass thesis. We are watching it closely as well. I’m just pointing out some crucial things. If you look carefully at what is actually being built, Absolics is building a facility of around 12,000 square meters and is in some pre-qualification discussions with AMD and AWS. That’s not HVM. That is a very expensive demonstration phase, which is totally normal. It happens a lot. Companies build pilot lines, run sample production, and do customer qualification runs. It’s an investment to stay in the technology race, but it’s not revenue-generating HVM deployment. Keep that in mind. The reality is that it’s a qualification facility running at a small fraction of the scale needed to move the industry and the economics. I will elaborate further on that a bit later. Semco and Absolics are substrate manufacturers, and they’re building capacity for a market whose architectural gateway has not yet opened. It actually has been delayed. And this is where people keep getting confused — substrate manufacturer ramp-up plans are not the same thing as equipment-supplier inflection. SEMCO and Absolics don’t decide whether glass enters the HVM stack at scale. They’re positioning themselves for it. Two very different things."}}}, "rest_id": "2045604137866551549", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "21560", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2045611455228047700", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "3842", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

39 M$ de Capitalisation boursière >Elle est directement en rapport avec la photonique, elle fabrique des atténuateurs Optiques Variables (VOA) MEMSCAP, à la base, c’est surtout un fournisseur de capteurs de pression de très haute précision pour l’aéronautique et le médical. Mais ils ont aussi un segment communications optiques. Ils fournissent des VOA basés sur technologie MEMS, utilisés pour gérer dynamiquement la puissance des signaux dans les réseaux fibre optique (datacenters, télécoms, amplis EDFA). Sur ce segment de niche, MEMSCAP est tout simplement leader mondial des puces MEMS VOA, avec une part de marché estimée à plus de 80% sur un marché d’environ 5 M$ au niveau puces, et 50 M$ au niveau produits finis. Pas l'ASML de la photonique mais une boite qui pourrait se rerater rapidement. D'ailleurs hier il y a eu un afflux plutôt anormal, ce qui me fait potentiellement penser à un positionnement d’un bon gros player. Si les microcaps continuent d'exploser, je parie que Memscap en bénéficiera probablement."}}}, "rest_id": "2045528106413199710", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "343592", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2045589392387920373", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "8000", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text: Marvell $MRVL is going strong into Photonics 🧐 Looks to me they want to control as much as possible of the supply chain (remem. my thesis about the Marvell - $POET deal, that perhaps it's only interested in the Interposer System, NOT in the POET Transceivers ==> no $SIVE Lasers)

Marvell $MRVL just announced it has agreed to acquire Polariton Technologies, 

"a developer of high-speed, low-power plasmonics-based silicon photonics devices"

Terms of the deal were not disclosed

text:
THE OPTICAL PHOTONICS BOTTLENECK...🧐

​As AI clusters blast past the physical limits of copper interconnects, the bottleneck is shifting hard into high-speed optics.

​These are the key players building the essential layers of the AI optical stack:

$AAOI - Transceiver Layer

Dominating with vertically integrated U.S.-based InP laser manufacturing. Already landed a major 1.6T order ($200M+) from a leading hyperscaler, plus strong 800G follow-on business.

$CRDO - Connectivity Layer

Powering high-speed links via AECs and advanced silicon. 
The DustPhotonics acquisition brings silicon photonics in-house, a massive edge as copper constraints intensify.

$LITE - Laser & Switching

Leader in EMLs and optical circuit switching. 
Backed by a $2B strategic investment from $NVDA with an optical switch backlog stretching into 2028.

$COHR - InP Infrastructure

Addressing the critical Indium Phosphide bottleneck. 
Holds a $2B $NVDA commitment and is scaling capacity aggressively for 1.6T+ networking.

$CIEN - Transport Layer

The backbone that ties everything together. 
Their solutions enable hyperscalers to move enormous AI traffic between data centers as clusters scale to gigawatt levels.

$FN - Manufacturing Backbone

The "picks-and-shovels" partner. As the reliable assembly giant for optical modules, they fuel the entire ramp where speed and scale determine the winners.

$MRVL - DSP & Electro-Optics

Strengthening the infrastructure with PAM DSPs and custom chips. 
The Celestial AI deal and NVLink exposure place them at the heart of next-gen cluster design.

$AEHR - Testing & Reliability

Providing essential wafer-level burn-in (FOX-XP). 
Recently secured a major leader in networking for 1.6T production ramp (critical for hyperscale reliability).

The transition from copper is no longer optional 😬
Anyway these stocks are volatile, with risks around customer concentration, production ramps (InP/SiPho), and high valuations. DYOR. NFA.

text:
$MRVL just made a move in the AI infrastructure race, acquiring Polariton Technologies to lock down the shift to 3.2T optical connectivity. 

​Why this is a big deal:
• ​Adds ultra-fast, low-power 
  plasmonics-based silicon photonics 
  to their stack.
• ​Solves the massive bandwidth and 
   power bottlenecks in next-gen AI 
   data centers.
• ​Positions Marvell perfectly as 
  architectures evolve beyond the 
  current 1.6T standard.

​Data centers need pure speed without melting the grid. Marvell is buying the exact tech to make it happen. 

$MRVL up 1.5% AH.

Laser Photonics Corp received a notice on April 20, 2026, from Nasdaq stating it failed to comply with listing rules due to not filing its Form 10-K for 2025, but subsequently filed the form, resolving the issue by April 21, 2026. The matter is now closed and the company has regained compliance with Nasdaq regulations.

text:
Copper Wall thesis is sharp—the shift to silicon photonics/CPO + quantum is real for AI power efficiency.

$5 each into the 12 stocks weekly = $60/wk, ~$15,600 over 5 years.

Silicon photonics market is forecast to grow 25-30% CAGR to ~$9-11B by 2030 (multiple sources). Quantum adds 20-40% upside potential.

In a strong AI adoption scenario, this basket (heavy on semis/photonics leaders like TSM/AVGO/MRVL + speculative IONQ) could compound 20-35% annualized on average—turning it into $28k-$45k by 2031 via DCA.

High volatility/risk though—some names are small-cap bets. Solid long-term play if the buildout accelerates. I'll watch updates. 🚀

text:
A lot of people seem to think photonics has already had its big run.
They’re missing the next leg.

Stocks like $AAOI and $LITE exploded on the current 800G pluggable wave — a lot of that growth is now priced in.

The real next chapter is CPO - (Co-Packaged Optics).

CPO solves the massive power and heat problems in AI data centers way better than today’s pluggables. Hyperscalers are already designing their future clusters around it. The big ramp will be 2027–2028.

The companies that actually enable CPO — $SIVE (critical lasers), $SOI (substrates), $TSEM (foundry), $AEHR (testing) — are still trading at valuations that don’t reflect how big this shift can become.

CPO is the layer where the next major move in photonics will come from IMO.

text:
Been digging through European photonics the last few weeks.

Most of you already know $AIXA and $SOI. Both still have room.

Getting a lot of questions about $LPK.DE lately so I want to address it directly.

LPKF is the laser process pioneer for glass substrates. Intel, Samsung, and TSMC are all piloting glass as the next-generation AI packaging material. Glass handles heat, density, and integrated optics better than anything else on the roadmap.

The bottleneck is simple. Glass cracks when you drill it. LPKF's LIDE process is one of a very small number of technologies that can structure glass at production scale without cracking. No glass substrate ecosystem gets built without a tool like theirs in the line.

Management's own words on the recent call: the question is no longer whether glass breakthrough in advanced packaging happens, but when the ramp-up begins.

There are two more names attached to this supply chain that I am still researching and not ready to publish on yet.

One is the integrated metrology sitting inside $AIXA's reactors. Trades like a sleepy industrial despite the direct AI attachment.

One is the micro-cap making the actual glass wafers. Up 200% already and still small enough that real revenue ramps break it wide open.

Besi trades at 77x EBITDA on the advanced packaging theme. These trade at fractions of that one layer upstream.

H/T to @AlmaCap114204 who found this one early.

More to come.

Most of you already know $AIXA and $SOI. Both still have room. Getting a lot of questions about $LPK.DE lately so I want to address it directly. LPKF is the laser process pioneer for glass substrates. Intel, Samsung, and TSMC are all piloting glass as the next-generation AI packaging material. Glass handles heat, density, and integrated optics better than anything else on the roadmap. The bottleneck is simple. Glass cracks when you drill it. LPKF's LIDE process is one of a very small number of technologies that can structure glass at production scale without cracking. No glass substrate ecosystem gets built without a tool like theirs in the line. Management's own words on the recent call: the question is no longer whether glass breakthrough in advanced packaging happens, but when the ramp-up begins. There are two more names attached to this supply chain that I am still researching and not ready to publish on yet. One is the integrated metrology sitting inside $AIXA's reactors. Trades like a sleepy industrial despite the direct AI attachment. One is the micro-cap making the actual glass wafers. Up 200% already and still small enough that real revenue ramps break it wide open. Besi trades at 77x EBITDA on the advanced packaging theme. These trade at fractions of that one layer upstream. H/T to @AlmaCap114204 who found this one early. More to come."}}}, "rest_id": "2044883190188568579", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "138263", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
And another one!!

$TER Teradyne has acquired TestInsight, a leader in semiconductor test development, validation, and conversion software.

So they first acquired my former employer Quantifi Photonics 🇳🇿, then my other former employer MutliLane 🇱🇧 in a JV, and now TestInsight 🇮🇱.

Teradyne picked up TestInsight, a software outfit that has been around since 1999 and sits right between chip design and ATE production test (pattern conversion, validation and virtual testing). 

They have been the default software partner for both Teradyne and Advantest (sayonara 👋🏻) since 2001, which covers roughly 90% of the SoC test market. 

For the MultiLane JV and Quantifi, the read is complementary, not overlapping: Quantifi brought optical test, MultiLane handles high-speed electrical and SerDes, and TestInsight now adds the upstream software layer. Lined up like that, it is a pretty clean roll-up aimed at owning the full test stack for AI accelerators, switch ASICs and CPO. 

Advantest $ATEYY is the one that loses here. They just watched a neutral partner get absorbed by their main competitor.​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​

Full press release from Teradyne below:

text:
Interesting take on the interconnect bottleneck. Photonics is indeed the physics play for scaling beyond copper limits—lower latency, power per bit, and heat as racks densify for AI training/inference. 

Data movement at exascale is the next constraint after raw FLOPs. COHR's optical tech sits right in that transfer layer. Capital cycles rotate to the real chokepoints. What's your edge on timing the shift?

Because copper-based systems hit limits:

-Heat
-Signal loss
-Power inefficiency
 
Photonics solves that. It allows data centers to scale without melting down. That’s where $COHR sits.
Right inside the transfer layer.

This is physics. You can’t scale AI if data can’t move efficiently.

text:
As models scale, something breaks:

-Bandwidth
-Latency
-Power per bit

That’s the real constraint forming. 
This is where photonics matters.

Light moves data faster
-More efficiently
-With less heat

That’s the shift most people haven’t priced in yet:
electrical → optical

text:
With a background in atomic precision measurements, I'm more optics, quantum sensing, and photonics "focused" in my current role, obviously for other applications, but thought if there was an intersection somewhere, some of the signatures of the Chronoflux could be probed with the sensors under development.

I haven't worked on Casimir devices yet, but they are on my radar. I am interested in phononic photonic resonators, especially in relation to tests of Orch OR, as well as PIC frequency combs for other sensing applications. So there's definitely some overlap.

Would be nice if you could implement the equations function in this document 🙏

The two Substack articles profile Nynomic AG as a German photonics holding company with LayTec as its key subsidiary, a quasi-monopolist in in-situ metrology for MOCVD reactors and etch processes powering AI data center lasers (InP) and power electronics (GaN). They highlight restructuring savings lifting 2026 margins to 6-8%, vertical integration across the value chain, and massive TAM expansion from optical transceivers and co-packaged optics. My analysis sees strong moats from proprietary databases, OEM lock-ins with Aixtron (90%+ share), and recurring software upsells despite past guidance misses and cyclical risks. Growth potential is substantial at 3-5x for LayTec revenue by 2028 driven by AI buildout, with Nynomic re-rating to peer multiples implying 2-3x stock upside. This stems from validated photonics bottlenecks via NVIDIA investments and staggered order cycles already showing Q4 2025 inflection.

text:
$GFS GlobalFoundries Inc. (GFS) Q4 FY2025 Post-Earnings Debrief

https://t.co/vZ2dYs7a7E

Thesis: GlobalFoundries delivered a Q4 FY2025 print that pushes the underwriting debate away from a mature-node recovery and toward whether silicon photonics, optical-module and advanced-packaging capabilities, CID growth, and geographically resilient specialty capacity can support a structurally higher-growth and higher-margin model. The strongest proof that this shift is already happening is that 2025 was the first full year with more than 60% of revenue outside smart mobile devices and automotive plus CID reached a record one-third of revenue, while elevated FY2026 capex and lower FCF conversion remain the central execution risk.

-Q1 2026 revenue, gross margin, and diluted EPS versus the $1.625B / 27% / $0.35 guide framework
-Investor Day detail on the silicon photonics bridge, including pluggables versus CPO timing, module-yield milestones, and the 40% gross-margin path
-Whether silicon photonics remains overwhelmingly pluggables-driven in 2026 while keeping the end-2028 $1B run-rate target credible
-Whether Malta optical-module flow, advanced packaging, and 200mm/300mm dual-region capacity support faster revenue conversion without yield disruption
-Whether the structural mix shift, now above 60% non-mobile revenue and one-third auto plus CID, keeps widening in 2026
-Whether government-support offsets, customer prepayments, and corridor-level debottlenecking keep FY2026 net capex and FCF conversion within the guided algorithm

text:
The 3-year rule holds for commoditizable bottlenecks: DRAM, NAND, legacy logic. It breaks for capital-intensive or IP-protected ones: ASML EUV is 9 years and counting, TSMC leading-edge over a decade, Soitec/Shin-Etsu on SOI wafers 15+ years. Photonics supply chain fits the second bucket, not the first.

On 'meme-like' valuations, the right benchmark isn't Street targets, it's what the market already pays for rerated peers. Coherent trades at ~8.4x EV/Sales, growing 22% Y/Y, with Nvidia owning $2B of equity. That's the anchor for a fully-discovered photonics name this cycle.

Checking the supply chain against it: Soitec ~4x EV/Sales (50% discount to COHR), IQE ~6x (in-play takeover), LPKF ~2x. Those are meaningful discounts to the rerated comp, not premiums.

Where you're right: names that have run 500%+ in months on sub-$50M revenue can't be defended on peer-relative grounds either. Those are real outliers worth separating from the broader supply-chain story.

Duration holds, most of the supply chain trades at discounts to the rerated peer, some individual names are genuinely overextended. Bottleneck durability and single-name multiples aren't the same question.

text:
Most finance Twitter talks about semiconductors.

Almost nobody on finance Twitter has actually built one.

Trade Whisperer has 21 years in HBM, DRAM, and NAND plus graduate research in silicon photonics and lasers.

His AMA is open right now.

If you have any real exposure to $MU, $SNDK, $TSM, or the broader semi complex, this is a rare window.

Here's why it matters.

The memory thesis driving Micron's projected out-earning of Amazon and Meta by 2027 sits on one question.

Can Samsung, SK Hynix, and Micron ramp HBM supply fast enough to capture current AI demand without triggering the classic memory glut cycle?

Analysts have views. Sell-side has models. Bulls and bears argue on podcasts.

Almost none of them have ever been inside a fab.

Trade Whisperer answers questions like "how long does HBM qualification actually take" and "why can't China catch up" from the engineering floor rather than the spreadsheet.

From the thread already running:

China catching up to HBM is "extremely hard. It's a moving target. If they ever figure out HBM3, others will be producing HBM4E by then."

Fab expansions "take decades for 100% utilization." The supply glut fear is priced for a cycle that can't physically happen as fast as the market assumes.

He's bullish on SK Hynix over Samsung specifically because of HBM3E leadership. That's not a trading view. That's an engineer's view on who actually makes the best chip right now.

These are the kind of reads that generate edge in semi positions.

Not because he's predicting prices. Because he's explaining physical constraints that determine what the prices eventually have to reflect.

If you hold MU or SNDK or any semi name, spend 20 minutes reading his replies.

Ask him something specific. Depth of qualification cycles. HBM4 timeline. Silicon photonics for interconnect. How packaging capacity actually constrains supply.

Finance Twitter is full of people who read earnings transcripts.

A semi engineer with 21 years of HBM experience doing an AMA is a different tier of information.

Use it while it's open.

Follow for more signals on when someone who actually knows something is worth listening to in public.

Any interesting recent papers on this terahertz + photonics combo you’d recommend? Always fun chatting with someone who sees the potential too! ✨

It is the company’s 100mm InP fab in Glasgow 🏭 Sivers owns and operates one of the few independent III-V photonic foundries with both advanced development capability and meaningful production capacity. This is not just an R&D line, but a qualified InP100 manufacturing platform with end-to-end capabilities — from epitaxy design and wafer processing to automated testing and qualification. The platform includes:  • Up to 5,000 wafer starts per year  • Automated test capacity of more than 2 million lasers per month  • ~$17M capex to expand toward ~$150M annual revenue capacity What makes this especially interesting is the etched-facet process with on-wafer optical coatings and testing. Rather than relying on slower, less efficient post-cleave workflows, Sivers can process, coat, and test on the full wafer before dicing — improving yield, cost, cycle time, and precision. Strategically, that matters. The in-house fab gives Sivers control over development, prototyping, qualification, and early-to-mid volume manufacturing, while partners like WIN Semiconductors provide a path to ultra-high-volume scale when needed. That combination supports the company’s shift from 1-to-1 to one-to-many: design once, qualify once, and sell repeatedly with known specs, shorter lead times, and better operating leverage. In an industry where many photonics players are fabless, owning this specialized manufacturing capability is a quiet but powerful advantage. Underrated foundation for $SIVE. $AAOI is good for photonics and $CRDO for connectivity. Also $CLS and $COHR which does it all, and $AEHR for testing every semiconductor.

Software it's clearly $PLTR and $APP upside is massive as well as $NOW.

Defense - Drones are probably a better bet. I like $SWMR after the IPO.. price is more attractive with Erik Prince leading it. His connections and experience is bar none the best. Swarmer software runs on a drone as well increasing the TAM 100 fold.

Health - I was against this initially but the rise of peptides is real, so $HIMS and $IBRX for cancer in biotech.

Space - $PL (Google backed) and $RKLB is great. $ASTS is gonna have too much competition for me.

Robotics.. $TSLA - People need to start making that connection and just go long.

Insurance - I couldn't care less about. All scams. Probably United Healthcare though.

Cybersecurity is gonna be $CRWD - they're onboard for Anthropics company leadership board for security concerns around AI guardrails.

Quantum... still early for me to go all in, but $IONQ $INFQ are solid bets.

text:
No, not really similar in core tech. 

VIGO Photonics makes high-sensitivity HgCdTe (MCT) uncooled IR detectors/sensors for MWIR/LWIR detection—used in missiles, gas sensing, defense, and space (e.g. Curiosity rover). 

$LPTH focuses on IR optics (chalcogenide BlackDiamond lenses) + complete thermal imaging cameras/systems. Complementary pieces of the IR chain, not the same detection tech. 

(From the infographic + company sites)

@Frenchie_ Photonics is the angle, but the real story is aerospace.
MEMSCAP's pressure sensors go into planes and rockets - defense spending is booming and that's the actual catalyst here.

text: Photonics are taking off because most are small caps. They are easily modulated by the sudden investments that come in. Now having said that, the need for optics is REAL (reserach Ultra Rubin). Whole suupply chain is trying to figure how important they will become in the next 5 years. You need lasers but you need special materials for lasers but you also need special Co-Packaged-Optics for these to work in tandem with Silicon Chips... now you need more Pure Fiber Optics glass... now you need Optics Foundry as well. So yeah, the whole chain is moving. It's hype/real depending which company we are taking about. If psychology is what you're seeking stay invested on easy to understand optics players. I am staying with $LITE $COHR $GLW. $TSM $ASX for CPO.

text:
$sive $sivef 1. The “Holy Grail”: Blood Glucose Laser & Apple
The speculation that Sivers’ lasers (via its subsidiary Sivers Photonics) could be integrated into a future Apple Watch represents the single most significant potential catalyst.
Strategic Rationale
Apple has spent over a decade developing non-invasive glucose monitoring using silicon photonics.
Sivers is one of the few companies capable of delivering the high-performance indium phosphide (InP) lasers required to penetrate the skin and measure glucose levels in interstitial fluid.
Status as of 2026
While Apple never confirms suppliers in advance, Sivers has disclosed collaboration with a “Fortune 100” medical technology customer.
If this customer is Apple, the resulting production volumes would be transformative for the company.

"Tm90ZVR3ZWV0OjIwNDU4ODU1MTI2OTE2NDIzNjg=", "media": {"inline_media": []}, "richtext": {"richtext_tags": []}, "text": "THE OPTICAL PHOTONICS BOTTLENECK As AI clusters scale past copper’s physical limits, the bottleneck shifts to optical & these are the companies building that layer across the stack: 1. $AAOI building the transceiver layer of the AI network through vertically integrated U.S.-based InP laser manufacturing. It has already secured over $200M in its first volume 1.6T order from one hyperscale customer followed by another $124M in 800G orders from a second. 2. $AEHR building the reliability layer for the optical & AI hardware stack through burn-in & test systems. It just received a record $41M follow-on order from its lead hyperscale customer reinforcing the idea that Sonoma is becoming a key production burn-in platform for high-power AI ASICs. 3. $CRDO building the connectivity layer that helps AI clusters move data faster through active electrical cables, retimers & high-speed interconnect silicon. The DustPhotonics acquisition also extends that platform into silicon photonics before copper becomes a real constraint. 4. $LITE building the laser layer of the AI optical stack through EMLs, optical components & optical switching exposure. The setup is backed by a $2B $NVDA strategic investment & optical circuit switch backlog above $400M with orders reportedly extending through 2028. 5. $VIAV building the testing & validation layer of the optical stack through network instrumentation & photonics measurement tools. It is the picks-and-shovels layer of the transition because every high-speed optical buildout still needs to be tested regardless of which transceiver vendor wins. 6. $COHR building one of the core photonics bottlenecks through indium phosphide lasers, optical engines & communications components tied to next-gen AI networking. It also has a $2B $NVDA strategic investment behind it & is doubling InP device capacity into the 1.6T ramp. 7. $MRVL building the DSP & optical infrastructure layer through electro-optics, PAM DSPs, interconnect silicon & custom networking chips. The Celestial AI deal & NVLink Fusion exposure both strengthen its position as photonics becomes more central to AI cluster design."}}}, "rest_id": "2045885512922419251", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "294825", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2047051196981223802", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "141291", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
Marvell $MRVL just announced it has agreed to acquire Polariton Technologies, 

"a developer of high-speed, low-power plasmonics-based silicon photonics devices"

Terms of the deal were not disclosed

$MRVL ... Marvell to acquire Polariton Technologies to advance next-gen silicon photonics and optical interconnects; financial terms not disclosed (157.32+6.01)
MRVL announced the acquisition of Polariton Technologies, a developer of high-speed, low-power plasmonics-based silicon photonics devices. The acquisition strengthens Marvell's optical technology portfolio by adding advanced modulation capabilities that enable continued scaling in bandwidth, power efficiency, and integration for next-generation coherent and optical interconnect platforms.

text:
$OPTX 2 o'clock balloon comment. If you started a bit in Syntec Optics, notice Ciena.. as $CIEN move up by several X.. well, photonics is what relatively unknown OPTX does.. to connect photonically. And Syntec Optics is in space, data-centers and weapons.. optical photonic connectivity. 

Let's see what it can doing in the weeks and months ahead... notably tightly controlled by one man (the CEO) and has Warrants that have to trade over $18 for 20 consecutive days before  $OPTX can call them in (very important); so I imagine the buyer of common expects to see the common move to 18 or higher (typically such deals go 10-30% above the conversion level at least); and $OPTXW (the Warrants would go anywhere from 4 to 10.. depending how high OPTX goes. We'll see, but with Anduril and Amazon ( $AMZN ) likely involved (one for the U.S. Army and the other for the Kuiper satellite LEO -low earth orbit- program) .. I imagine OPTX moves higher rapidly at some point. Zero analyst coverage; so that encourages me that the real action is ahead. It's just one of the newer picks this year; and doesn't at all distract from others we like, hold or follow... $ONDS, $QBTS (very strong in Quantum now) or $BBAI (seems to be positioning for a move.. finally if it happens). 

Or $RDW and others.. our diversified picks are mostly within what I broadly call 'Global Defense, Security & Drones' (but let's include space as part of that); and n that sense we are concentrated in what 'serious' money is now more interested in than old-guard big-caps. Starting with $IONQ (sold for good gain) and $QBTS (sold Warrants kept common) back in 2025; we added ONDS and others along the way. We embraced this overall theme well before others and patienly fought through ups-and-downs and believe highs are ahead not beyind for virtually all of these. My humble opinion.
Can $POET rock out just because it’s one of the very few low priced Photonics Space names? $LITE $AAOI $MRVL $AXTI

One integrated system.

Technical docs already submitted to U.S. Special Operations Command and Naval Special Warfare.

This is not conceptual. This is prototype-proven directed energy, purpose-built for defense contracts.

Laser Photonics is not just cleaning rust off engines anymore - they are building the future of airspace security.

$LASE On the radar. In the portfolio.
